title: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman reports that on the opening day of Governor Greg Abbott's July 2021 special session, Texas State Rep. Bryan Slaton refiled HB103 to immediately abolish abortion in Texas, while warning that pro-life leaders like Rep. Stephanie Klick are positioned to repeat the prior session's tactic of using useless incremental bills as political cover to kill the abolition bill.
audiences: Texas abolitionist activist tracking the special session
Pro-life voter unsure whether incremental bills count as abolition
Republican Party of Texas precinct chair or platform delegate
Skeptical journalist covering the Texas legislature

questions.question: What bill did Bryan Slaton file on the opening day of the July 2021 Texas special session, and what does it do?
questions.audience: Texas abolitionist activist tracking the special session
questions.answer: On the opening day of the special session called by Governor Abbott, Slaton filed HB103, formally titled the Abolition of Abortion Through Equal Protection for All Unborn Children Act, which would extend equal protection of the law to preborn children rather than merely regulating abortion.
questions.quote: On the opening day of the session, State Rep. Bryan Slaton filed [HB103](https://capitol.texas.gov/BillLookup/History.aspx?LegSess=871&Bill=HB103), the Abolition of Abortion Through Equal Protection for All Unborn Children Act.

questions.question: What did Slaton already do during the regular session to fight for preborn children, and how does that establish his track record?
questions.audience: Texas abolitionist activist tracking the special session
questions.answer: During his freshman regular session Slaton filed HB3326 to immediately give preborn children equal protection, attempted to amend House rules to force a vote on abolition before any vote naming bridges or roads, and filed an amendment to a counter-productive pro-life bill to convert it into immediate abolition.
questions.quote: Slaton fought valiantly for preborn children in Texas during his freshman session in the House — introducing [HB3326](https://legiscan.com/TX/text/HB3326/2021) which would have immediately provided preborn children the equal protection of the law, [motioning to amend](https://www.facebook.com/watch/AbolishAbortionTexas/) House rules to force a vote on abolishing abortion before any vote to name bridges or roads, and [filing an amendment](https://www.facebook.com/1091574574227150/videos/798420244378197) to a counter-productive pro-life bill to make it immediately abolish abortion.

questions.question: Who killed HB3326 in the regular session, and how was it killed?
questions.audience: Pro-life voter unsure whether incremental bills count as abolition
questions.answer: Public Health Committee chair Stephanie Klick, a self-identified pro-life Republican, denied HB3326 a committee hearing, and her office reportedly told constituents falsehoods about the bill to justify the move even though three other Republicans had coauthored it.
questions.quote: HB3326 was coauthored by Reps. James White, Kyle Biedermann, and Tom Oliverson, but it was denied a committee hearing by Public Health Committee chair Stephanie Klick, a pro-life Republican. Klick’s office went as far as telling her constituents [blatant lies](https://abolishabortiontx.org/2021/04/21/responding-to-misinformaton-about-hb-3326/) about the bill in an attempt to defend her actions.
questions.quote_note: Two-sentence quote pulled together because the second sentence is the explicit charge about how the bill was killed.

questions.question: What strategy did the pro-life establishment use to protect Klick after she killed the abolition bill, and where else has this tactic shown up?
questions.audience: Skeptical journalist covering the Texas legislature
questions.answer: Texas Right to Life and other pro-life groups rallied around Klick by publishing celebratory pieces touting unrelated incremental bills she let through committee; Silberman says the same playbook of using small pro-life bills as political cover for committee chairs who kill abolition has been deployed in Oklahoma, Missouri, and other states.
questions.quote: This same tactic has been applied in Oklahoma, Missouri, and other states where abolition bills are introduced. Abolition bills were assigned to the same committee as a bunch of useless pro-life bills, and the pro-life bills were used as political cover for the committee chair who killed the abolition bill. Be on the lookout for this strategy again in the special session.

questions.question: What did Governor Abbott actually call for instead of abolition, and how does Silberman characterize it?
questions.audience: Pro-life voter unsure whether incremental bills count as abolition
questions.answer: Abbott's special-session call asked for legislation strengthening reporting of abortions and abortion complications and ensuring informed consent for abortion-inducing drugs—measures Silberman dismisses as politically useful theater that cannot save lives because they remain within the bounds of Roe and Casey.
questions.quote: Instead of the abolition of abortion, Governor Abbott called for legislation that “strengthens the laws applicable to the reporting of abortions and abortion complications,” whatever that is supposed to accomplish, and “ensures that no abortion-inducing drugs are provided unless there is voluntary and informed consent.” Like all pro-life legislation that can be upheld within *Roe* and *Casey*, this bill is completely useless, but it allows pro-life politicians and lobbyists to look like they’re doing something, which is politics 101.

questions.question: Why is it significant that Stephanie Klick is the primary House author of Abbott's preferred special-session bill?
questions.audience: Skeptical journalist covering the Texas legislature
questions.answer: Silberman argues it is the same recycled cover-fire pattern: the legislator who killed equal-protection abolition is now positioned to pass an incremental bill that will be marketed as a pro-life win, allowing her to be hailed as a hero by the same organizations even as roughly 110,000 Texas children are killed annually.
questions.quote: Unsurprisingly, the primary House author of the bill is none other than Stephanie Klick. Her useless bill has a good chance of passing and if it does, the Representative who made sure that 110,000 babies are murdered in Texas will be touted as a pro-life hero by the pro-life organizations once again. This is one of the reasons it is so important to oppose incremental pro-life legislation.

questions.question: Why hasn't HB103 been assigned to a committee yet, and what's the irony Silberman points out?
questions.answer: Texas House Democrats had flown to Washington, D.C. to lobby against the U.S. Senate filibuster, which paralyzed Texas House activity and effectively functioned as a filibuster of its own—an irony Silberman calls 'too rich.'
questions.quote: As for HB103, it has yet to be assigned to a committee. Part of the holdup is due to Texas democrats [flying to D.C.](https://www.salisburypost.com/2021/06/04/byron-york-in-texas-democrats-thwart-will-of-majority/), essentially filibustering all Texas House activity, to lobby against the U.S. Senate filibuster. The irony really is too rich.

questions.question: How does the Republican Party of Texas platform relate to HB103, and why does Silberman emphasize the platform's specificity?
questions.audience: Republican Party of Texas precinct chair or platform delegate
questions.answer: The Texas GOP again named the abolition of abortion as a legislative priority for the special session and explicitly called for passage of a bill modeled after HB3326—language deliberately precise enough to exclude pro-life groups who have been redefining 'abolition' to cover their incremental bills.
questions.quote: Texas abolitionists have worked hard in recent years to [make abolition a legislative priority of the party](https://freethestates.org/2020/07/texas-abolitionists-make-abortions-abolition-an-official-tx-gop-priority/index.html), and accordingly, the Republican Party of Texas has once again [publicized](https://freethestates.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/07/Screen-Shot-2021-07-15-at-2.39.37-PM-1.png) the abolition of abortion as a legislative priority of the party going into the special session. Even better, they specifically called for the passage of a bill like HB3326 from the previous session.

questions.question: How are Texas pro-life organizations co-opting the language of abolition, and what specific examples does the article cite?
questions.audience: Pro-life voter unsure whether incremental bills count as abolition
questions.answer: Texas Right to Life has rebranded its set of incremental bills as the 'Texas Abolition Strategy,' and Texas Alliance for Life has publicly described its trigger bill—which Silberman says actually makes Texas abortion statutes more unjust—as a bill 'to abolish abortion in Texas,' both attempts to capture the abolitionist label without supporting equal protection.
questions.quote: Texas Right to Life has called their collection of incremental bills the [Texas Abolition Strategy](https://freethestates.org/2019/09/faux-abolitionism/index.html). Texas Alliance for Life [posted](https://www.facebook.com/TexasAllianceForLife/photos/10159694796246807) on Facebook that their trigger bill, which actually makes Texas abortion statutes [even more unjust than they already are](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8X2537Gui9g), is a bill to “abolish abortion in Texas.”

questions.question: What concrete actions does the article ask readers to take?
questions.audience: Texas abolitionist activist tracking the special session
questions.answer: Readers are told to follow the Abolish Abortion Texas and Free the States Facebook pages for updates on HB103, sign up for email lists to bypass declining social media reach, do conversion-style activism using resources like the 'Are You An Abolitionist' pamphlet, and become monthly donors to Free the States.
questions.quote: Stay tuned to the Facebook pages of [Abolish Abortion Texas](https://www.facebook.com/AbolishAbortionTexas) and [Free the States](https://www.facebook.com/FreeTheStates) for further updates about HB103!

related.title: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
related.reason: Direct prequel covering Slaton's original HB3326 filing in the regular session that this article repeatedly references.

related.title: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
related.reason: Establishes the recurring 110,000-lives framing Silberman uses when describing Klick's role.

related.title: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
related.reason: Background for the article's claim that the Republican Party of Texas has formally adopted abolition as a priority and explicitly called for an HB3326-style bill.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
related.reason: Develops the broader critique of Greg Abbott and the Texas pro-life establishment that underlies Silberman's reading of the special session call.

related.title: Don't Fall For Texas Right to Life's Faux Abolitionism
related.reason: Expands on the 'Texas Abolition Strategy' language-theft point Silberman makes about Texas Right to Life.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Backs up the article's swipe at Texas Alliance for Life's trigger-bill positioning and Roe/Casey-bound strategy.

related.title: BIG: Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against GOP Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
related.reason: Follow-up that shows the political consequences for Stephanie Klick after she killed the abolition bill discussed here.
